    JavierBravo Bernal

  // 01
  About Me
  
    
      
        I'm a Mechatronics Engineer with a strong background in
        automation, control systems, and electrical engineering. My career started
        in Ecuador, where I worked as an Electrical Engineer in
        a ceramic plant — programming PLCs, maintaining industrial motors, and
        keeping production lines running. I then moved into
        Quality Control, evaluating X-ray medical equipment
        against national regulations for Inglemed.
      
      
        Today, based in New York City, I work as a
        Service Engineer at Ecolab, designing digital maintenance
        systems and troubleshooting complex electromechanical equipment for
        enterprise clients. In parallel, I'm completing my
        M.S. in Applied Artificial Intelligence at Baruch College,
        combining my engineering foundation with machine learning, deep learning,
        and data analytics.
      
      
        My goal is to bridge the gap between physical systems and intelligent
        software — building solutions that are both technically robust and
        data-informed.
      
    
    
      Education
      
        M.S. Applied Artificial IntelligenceBaruch College · 2025–Present
        B.S. Mechatronics EngineeringTecnológico de Monterrey · 2017–2021
        Azure Data Fundamentals (DP-900)Microsoft Certification

  // 03
  Projects
  
    Each project below includes a full write-up with annotated code and analysis.
    More coming as the M.S. program progresses.
  
  
    
      PROJECT · 01
      Numismática México — Holiday Sales Strategy
      
        Tableau data story for a Mexican numismatics retailer. Identified the
        star product (~25% of Q4 profit), quantified ad lift (64%), and produced
        a focused regional marketing playbook.
      
      
        Tableau

        Storytelling
      
      View Project →

      Credit Risk Classification
      
        End-to-end scikit-learn pipeline benchmarking Logistic Regression,
        Random Forest, and SVM on the German Credit dataset with repeated
        stratified CV. ROC-AUC 0.78, with a discussion of the recall trade-off.
      
      
        Python
        scikit-learn
        Machine Learning
        Classification
      
      View Project →
    
    
      PROJECT · 03
      US Internal Migration & Congressional Reapportionment
      
        Explores ACS migration flow data to forecast state populations by 2030
        and model congressional seat reapportionment using Huntington-Hill method.
      
      
        R

          Designed a digital maintenance tracking system reducing downtime by 15%.
          Led troubleshooting and installation of complex electromechanical systems.
          Improved team productivity by 20% through mentoring and structured diagnostics.
          Collaborated with sales and operations to align technical solutions with client needs.

          Improved monthly revenue by 10% via demand forecasting and behavioral analytics.

title: Javier Bravo Bernal
text:
# =========================================
# CREDIT RISK - CLEAN PIPELINE
# =========================================

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_validate, RepeatedStratifiedKFold
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix


# =========================================
# 1. LOAD DATA
# =========================================
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/selva86/datasets/master/GermanCredit.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)

print("✅ Data loaded")


# =========================================
# 2. FIX TARGET (CORRECT WAY)
# =========================================
# Original dataset: 1 = good, 0 = bad
# We want: 0 = good, 1 = bad

df["credit_risk"] = df["credit_risk"].map({1: 0, 0: 1})

print("\nTarget distribution:")
print(df["credit_risk"].value_counts())


# =========================================
# 3. SPLIT DATA
# =========================================
X = df.drop("credit_risk", axis=1)
y = df["credit_risk"]

Xtrain, Xtest, ytrain, ytest = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, stratify=y, random_state=42
)


# =========================================
# 4. PREPROCESSING
# =========================================
numeric_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns
categorical_cols = X.select_dtypes(include="object").columns

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ("num", StandardScaler(), numeric_cols),
    ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"), categorical_cols)
])


# =========================================
# 5. MODELS
# =========================================
models = {
    "Logistic": LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced"),
    "RandomForest": RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, class_weight="balanced"),
    "SVM": SVC(probability=True)
}


# =========================================
# 6. CROSS VALIDATION
# =========================================
cv = RepeatedStratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, n_repeats=2, random_state=42)

scoring = ['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1', 'roc_auc']


results = []

for name, model in models.items():

    pipeline = Pipeline([
        ("prep", preprocessor),
        ("model", model)
    ])

    scores = cross_validate(
        pipeline,
        Xtrain,
        ytrain,
        cv=cv,
        scoring=scoring,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    print(f"\n🚀 {name}")
    print("------------------------")

    row = {"Model": name}

    for metric in scoring:
        val_score = np.mean(scores[f"test_{metric}"])
        print(f"{metric}: {val_score:.3f}")
        row[metric] = val_score

    results.append(row)


# =========================================
# 7. RESULTS TABLE
# =========================================
df_results = pd.DataFrame(results).sort_values(by="roc_auc", ascending=False)

print("\n📊 MODEL COMPARISON")
print(df_results)


# =========================================
# 8. FINAL MODEL (BEST ONE)
# =========================================
best_model_name = df_results.iloc[0]["Model"]
print(f"\n🏆 Best Model: {best_model_name}")

best_model = models[best_model_name]

final_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("prep", preprocessor),
    ("model", best_model)
])

final_pipeline.fit(Xtrain, ytrain)

y_pred = final_pipeline.predict(Xtest)


# =========================================
# 9. FINAL EVALUATION
# =========================================
print("\n📊 FINAL TEST PERFORMANCE")
print(classification_report(ytest, y_pred))

print("\nConfusion Matrix:")
print(confusion_matrix(ytest, y_pred))

✅ Data loaded

Target distribution:
credit_risk
0    700
1    300
Name: count, dtype: int64

🚀 Logistic
------------------------

precision: 0.495

roc_auc: 0.782

🚀 SVM
------------------------

recall: 0.319

📊 MODEL COMPARISON

2           SVM  0.748130   0.678272  0.318750  0.431021  0.782244
1  RandomForest  0.745006   0.677061  0.295833  0.409324  0.782059

🏆 Best Model: SVM

📊 FINAL TEST PERFORMANCE

           0       0.81      0.92      0.86       140
           1       0.72      0.48      0.58        60

title: Credit Risk Classification — German Credit Dataset
text:
← Back to Projects



  // Project 02 · Machine Learning · Classification
  Credit Risk ClassificationGerman Credit Dataset
  
    A clean, end-to-end scikit-learn pipeline that predicts whether a loan
    applicant is a credit risk. Compares Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and
    SVM with repeated stratified cross-validation across five metrics, and
    discusses the recall trade-off that matters most when the cost of a missed
    bad loan is asymmetric.
  
  
    Python
    scikit-learn
    Pandas
    Pipelines
    Cross-Validation
    Classification
  



  
    1,000
    Loan applications · 70/30 class split
  
  
    3
    Models benchmarked end-to-end
  
  
    0.782
    Best CV ROC-AUC (SVM)
  
  
    79%
    Final test accuracy
  




  Problem framing
  
    Credit risk classification is a textbook supervised learning problem with a
    non-textbook business consideration: the cost of the two error types
    is not symmetric. Approving a bad loan (false negative) is materially
    more expensive than rejecting a good one (false positive). That makes raw
    accuracy a poor single-metric optimizer — recall on the minority "bad credit"
    class and ROC-AUC are the more honest scorecards.
  

  The goal of this project was to:
  
    Build a reproducible, leakage-free preprocessing + modeling pipeline.
    Benchmark three model families across five metrics with proper CV.
    Pick a final model based on the right metric for the business, not the highest accuracy.
  

  Dataset
  
    The classic UCI German Credit dataset (1,000 observations, 20
    features — mix of numeric and categorical) loaded directly from a public
    mirror:
  

  url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/selva86/datasets/master/GermanCredit.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)

  
    The published target encoding is 1 = good, 0 = bad, which is
    unintuitive for "risk" — a bigger number should mean more risk. I flipped it
    so 1 = bad (positive class = the thing we want to detect):
  

  df["credit_risk"] = df["credit_risk"].map({1: 0, 0: 1})

  
    That puts the class distribution at 700 good / 300 bad — a meaningful but not
    extreme imbalance.
  

  
    
    Class distribution after target re-encoding — 70/30 split between good and bad credit.
  

  Pipeline design
  
    I used a single Pipeline per model so that all preprocessing
    happens inside each CV fold — no leakage from the test fold into the
    scaler or one-hot encoder.
  

  numeric_cols     = X.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns
categorical_cols = X.select_dtypes(include="object").columns

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ("num", StandardScaler(),                       numeric_cols),
    ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"), categorical_cols),
])

models = {
    "Logistic":     LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced"),
    "RandomForest": RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, class_weight="balanced"),
    "SVM":          SVC(probability=True),
}

  A few deliberate design choices:
  
    class_weight="balanced" on Logistic and RF to push them to actually try on the minority class, instead of collapsing to the majority-class predictor.
    handle_unknown="ignore" on the OHE — protects against unseen categories in test folds.
    stratify=y in the train/test split — preserves the class ratio.
  

  Cross-validation
  
    RepeatedStratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, n_repeats=2, random_state=42)
    — six total folds, stratified, evaluated on five metrics simultaneously:
  

  scoring = ['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1', 'roc_auc']
scores  = cross_validate(pipeline, Xtrain, ytrain, cv=cv,
                         scoring=scoring, n_jobs=-1)

  Results — model comparison

  



Model
Accuracy
Precision
Recall
F1
ROC-AUC




SVM
0.748
0.678
0.319
0.431
0.782


Random Forest
0.745
0.677
0.296
0.409
0.782


Logistic Regression
0.696
0.495
0.658
0.565
0.762





  
    
    Cross-validated metrics across five scorers. Note the recall axis — Logistic Regression dominates there.
  

  
    The headline result is not the headline. SVM and Random
    Forest win on accuracy, precision, and ROC-AUC. But Logistic Regression wins
    decisively on recall (0.658 vs. ~0.30) and F1
    — exactly the metrics that matter when the bad class is the one you're trying
    to catch.
  

  Final model — SVM on the test set
  
    The notebook selects on ROC-AUC and ships SVM, evaluated on the held-out test
    set (n=200):
  

                precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.81      0.92      0.86       140
           1       0.72      0.48      0.58        60

    accuracy                           0.79       200
    macro avg      0.77      0.70      0.72       200
weighted avg       0.78      0.79      0.78       200

  
    
    Final SVM confusion matrix on the test set. 31 bad applicants were missed (false negatives) — the costly error.
  

  In plain terms:
  
    129 / 140 good loans correctly approved (92% recall on class 0)
    29 / 60 bad loans correctly rejected (48% recall on class 1)
    31 bad loans approved by mistake — the most expensive cell in the matrix.
  

  Discussion
  
    A 79% accuracy SVM looks tidy on a slide, but if a bank deployed this model
    as-is it would still approve roughly half the bad applicants.
    The honest read of the experiment is:
  

  
    Choose by business metric, not default accuracy. For asymmetric-cost problems, recall on the positive class and the precision–recall trade-off at a chosen threshold beat any single number.
    Logistic Regression is the better starting point than the leaderboard implies. Its 0.66 recall vs. SVM's 0.32 means more risky applicants flagged, with only a small ROC-AUC penalty.
    Threshold tuning is the next obvious move. SVM's predict_proba outputs let you slide the decision threshold below 0.5 to trade precision for recall on class 1 — likely a much bigger win than swapping model families.
  

  Possible next steps
  
    Tune the decision threshold on the validation fold (target recall ≥ 0.70).
    Apply SMOTE / imbalanced-learn resampling inside the pipeline.
    Add SHAP feature attributions for explainability — required for any real credit context.
    Cost-sensitive learning with explicit (C_FN, C_FP) weights set by the business.
  

  Resources
  
    📥 Download the source notebook (.ipynb)
  




  Last Updated:
April 25, 2026
   ·  Javier Bravo Bernal  ·  Built with Quarto & R

title: Credit Risk Classification — German Credit Dataset
text:
Problem framing
Credit risk classification is a textbook supervised learning problem with a non-textbook business consideration: the cost of the two error types is not symmetric. Approving a bad loan (false negative) is materially more expensive than rejecting a good one (false positive). That makes raw accuracy a poor single-metric optimizer — recall on the minority “bad credit” class and ROC-AUC are the more honest scorecards.
The goal of this project was to:

Build a reproducible, leakage-free preprocessing + modeling pipeline.
Benchmark three model families across five metrics with proper CV.
Pick a final model based on the right metric for the business, not the highest accuracy.

title: Credit Risk Classification — German Credit Dataset
text:
Dataset
The classic UCI German Credit dataset (1,000 observations, 20 features — mix of numeric and categorical) loaded directly from a public mirror:

url = “https://raw.githubusercontent.com/selva86/datasets/master/GermanCredit.csv” df = pd.read_csv(url)

The published target encoding is 1 = good, 0 = bad, which is unintuitive for “risk” — a bigger number should mean more risk. I flipped it so 1 = bad (positive class = the thing we want to detect):

df[“credit_risk”] = df[“credit_risk”].map({1: 0, 0: 1})

That puts the class distribution at 700 good / 300 bad — a meaningful but not extreme imbalance.



Class distribution after target re-encoding — 70/30 split between good and bad credit.

title: Credit Risk Classification — German Credit Dataset
text:
Pipeline design
I used a single Pipeline per model so that all preprocessing happens inside each CV fold — no leakage from the test fold into the scaler or one-hot encoder.

numeric_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns categorical_cols = X.select_dtypes(include=“object”).columns
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([ (“num”, StandardScaler(), numeric_cols), (“cat”, OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown=“ignore”), categorical_cols),])
models = { “Logistic”: LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight=“balanced”), “RandomForest”: RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, class_weight=“balanced”), “SVM”: SVC(probability=True), }

A few deliberate design choices:

class_weight="balanced" on Logistic and RF to push them to actually try on the minority class, instead of collapsing to the majority-class predictor.
handle_unknown="ignore" on the OHE — protects against unseen categories in test folds.
stratify=y in the train/test split — preserves the class ratio.

title: Credit Risk Classification — German Credit Dataset
text:
Cross-validation
RepeatedStratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, n_repeats=2, random_state=42) — six total folds, stratified, evaluated on five metrics simultaneously:

scoring = [‘accuracy’, ‘precision’, ‘recall’, ‘f1’, ‘roc_auc’] scores = cross_validate(pipeline, Xtrain, ytrain, cv=cv, scoring=scoring, n_jobs=-1)

Final model — SVM on the test set
The notebook selects on ROC-AUC and ships SVM, evaluated on the held-out test set (n=200):

title: Credit Risk Classification — German Credit Dataset
text:
Discussion
A 79% accuracy SVM looks tidy on a slide, but if a bank deployed this model as-is it would still approve roughly half the bad applicants. The honest read of the experiment is:

Choose by business metric, not default accuracy. For asymmetric-cost problems, recall on the positive class and the precision–recall trade-off at a chosen threshold beat any single number.
Logistic Regression is the better starting point than the leaderboard implies. Its 0.66 recall vs. SVM’s 0.32 means more risky applicants flagged, with only a small ROC-AUC penalty.
Threshold tuning is the next obvious move. SVM’s predict_proba outputs let you slide the decision threshold below 0.5 to trade precision for recall on class 1 — likely a much bigger win than swapping model families.


Possible next steps

Tune the decision threshold on the validation fold (target recall ≥ 0.70).
Apply SMOTE / imbalanced-learn resampling inside the pipeline.
Add SHAP feature attributions for explainability — required for any real credit context.
Cost-sensitive learning with explicit (C_FN, C_FP) weights set by the business.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
← Back to Projects



  // Project 01 · Data Visualization & Marketing Analytics
  Numismática MéxicoYear-End Holiday Sales Strategy
  
    A Tableau-based data story analyzing four years of sales data for a Mexican
    numismatics retailer. The work identified the brand's "star product,"
    quantified the lift from paid advertising, mapped product preferences across
    states, and produced a focused marketing playbook for the Q4 holiday window.
  
  
    Tableau
    Data Visualization
    Marketing Analytics
    Storytelling
    Business Strategy
  



  
    $50K+
    Peak monthly profit (MXN) · Sept–Dec 2021
  
  
    25%
    Share of Q4 profits from one star product
  
  
    64%
    Star-product sales attributable to ads
  
  
    $23K
    Cumulative ad-driven profit · Central Region
  




  Context
  
    Numismática México is a retailer of commemorative Mexican coins and bills sold
    to collectors across the country. Like many specialty retail businesses, its
    revenue is heavily seasonal — the September-to-December window consistently
    delivers the strongest profit. The question driving this analysis was simple:
  

  
    How can the brand replicate (or beat) its 2021 holiday peak — and where
    should the marketing budget go?
  

  
    To answer it I built a multi-chapter Tableau story that walks a non-technical
    stakeholder from the high-level seasonality picture down to product- and
    state-level recommendations.
  

  The Data Story
  
    The Tableau workbook is structured as a six-chapter narrative. Each chapter
    isolates one decision-relevant insight, building toward the final
    recommendations.
  

  
    
    Story structure — six chapters from seasonality to regional ad impact.
  

  1 · Seasonality is the foundation
  
    The first chapter establishes that holiday sales are not an opinion but a
    consistent, multi-year pattern. From September to December 2021 monthly profit
    exceeded 50,000 MXN — the highest peak on record — confirming Q4 as the window
    where the marketing spend should be concentrated.
  

  
    
    Year-end 2021 recorded the highest sales profits on record.
  

  
    The natural counter-question — "what happens after the peak?" — is
    answered in the next chapter. January 2022 dropped to ~15K MXN, but that drop
    is also the moment paid advertising starts contributing for the first time.
  

  
    
    Sharp post-holiday drop — but also the first month with ad-supported sales.
  

  2 · Advertising changes the curve
  
    By March 2022 ad-supported sales overtook organic sales for the first time, and
    by late July 2022 ad-attributed profit cleared 20K MXN. This is the most
    important behavioral finding in the deck: paid advertising doesn't just add a
    flat lift, it shifts the slope of the recovery after the holiday peak.
  

  
    
    Ad-supported profits surpass organic, then exceed 20K MXN by late July 2022.
  

  3 · There is a star product
  
    Not every product contributed equally. The Commemorative 5, 10 & 40 Peso
    Bills & Coins Binder generated 42,466 MXN of profit
    between September and December 2021 — roughly 25% of the entire Q4
    total (175,826 MXN).
  

  
    
    The Commemorative Binder alone delivered ~25% of Sept–Dec 2021 profit.
  

  
    When advertising entered the picture, this product responded most strongly:
    ~64% of its post-launch sales are attributable to ads.
  

  
    
    After ads launched, ~64% of star-product sales were advertising-driven.
  

  
    The same product held the #1 position across every period studied — an unusual
    level of stability that makes it the natural anchor for any campaign.
  

  
    
    The same product held the top-selling position across all measurement periods.
  

  
    Caveat — Feb–Mar 2022 dip. Ad attribution on the star product
    dipped noticeably in this window. The recommendation includes a root-cause
    analysis before scaling spend, since campaign fatigue, creative issues, or
    platform-side attribution changes are all plausible explanations.
  

  
    
    Advertising impact dipped Feb–Mar 2022 — flagged for follow-up RCA.
  

  4 · Geography matters
  
    The national star product is not the top performer in every
    state. A state-by-state breakdown surfaces a second high-impact product that
    sits outside the national Top 3 — an untapped opportunity for regional
    campaigns.
  

  
    
    Best-selling product varies by state — the national star isn't always the local star.
  

  
    
    Two products with disproportionate impact — one of them outside the overall Top 3.
  

  
    Regional ad-driven profit is heavily concentrated. The Central Region alone has
    generated approximately 23K MXN in cumulative ad-attributed
    profit — a clear signal of where the next campaign dollar earns the most.
  

  
    
    Cumulative ad-driven profit by region — Central Region leads.
  

  Strategic Recommendations
  Three campaigns, sequenced by risk and confidence:

  
    
    The three-campaign playbook: star product, Top 5 replication, regional focus.
  

  
    
      Prioritize the star product in high-revenue states.
      Highest-confidence move; the product has demonstrated price-insensitivity and
      a strong ad lift on a customer base that already recognizes the brand.
    
    
      Replicate the January star-product playbook across the broader catalog.
      The proven-creative approach from the early-2022 campaign is the lowest-cost
      way to lift Top 5 products without rebuilding strategy from scratch.
    
    
      Run a Top-5 product campaign in high-revenue states.
      Combines (1) and (2) — uses the star product as the anchor and the Top 5 for
      cross-sell, targeting only the regions where the existing customer base
      already lifts ROAS.
    
  

  
    
    Why each recommendation works — the underlying business levers.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
Context
Numismática México is a retailer of commemorative Mexican coins and bills sold to collectors across the country. Like many specialty retail businesses, its revenue is heavily seasonal — the September-to-December window consistently delivers the strongest profit. The question driving this analysis was simple:

How can the brand replicate (or beat) its 2021 holiday peak — and where should the marketing budget go?

To answer it I built a multi-chapter Tableau story that walks a non-technical stakeholder from the high-level seasonality picture down to product- and state-level recommendations.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
The Data Story
The Tableau workbook is structured as a six-chapter narrative. Each chapter isolates one decision-relevant insight, building toward the final recommendations.



Story structure — six chapters from seasonality to regional ad impact.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
1 · Seasonality is the foundation
The first chapter establishes that holiday sales are not an opinion but a consistent, multi-year pattern. From September to December 2021 monthly profit exceeded 50,000 MXN — the highest peak on record — confirming Q4 as the window where the marketing spend should be concentrated.



Year-end 2021 recorded the highest sales profits on record.


The natural counter-question — “what happens after the peak?” — is answered in the next chapter. January 2022 dropped to ~15K MXN, but that drop is also the moment paid advertising starts contributing for the first time.



Sharp post-holiday drop — but also the first month with ad-supported sales.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
2 · Advertising changes the curve
By March 2022 ad-supported sales overtook organic sales for the first time, and by late July 2022 ad-attributed profit cleared 20K MXN. This is the most important behavioral finding in the deck: paid advertising doesn’t just add a flat lift, it shifts the slope of the recovery after the holiday peak.



Ad-supported profits surpass organic, then exceed 20K MXN by late July 2022.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
3 · There is a star product
Not every product contributed equally. The Commemorative 5, 10 & 40 Peso Bills & Coins Binder generated 42,466 MXN of profit between September and December 2021 — roughly 25% of the entire Q4 total (175,826 MXN).



The Commemorative Binder alone delivered ~25% of Sept–Dec 2021 profit.


When advertising entered the picture, this product responded most strongly: ~64% of its post-launch sales are attributable to ads.



After ads launched, ~64% of star-product sales were advertising-driven.


The same product held the #1 position across every period studied — an unusual level of stability that makes it the natural anchor for any campaign.



The same product held the top-selling position across all measurement periods.



Caveat — Feb–Mar 2022 dip. Ad attribution on the star product dipped noticeably in this window. The recommendation includes a root-cause analysis before scaling spend, since campaign fatigue, creative issues, or platform-side attribution changes are all plausible explanations.




Advertising impact dipped Feb–Mar 2022 — flagged for follow-up RCA.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
4 · Geography matters
The national star product is not the top performer in every state. A state-by-state breakdown surfaces a second high-impact product that sits outside the national Top 3 — an untapped opportunity for regional campaigns.



Best-selling product varies by state — the national star isn’t always the local star.





Two products with disproportionate impact — one of them outside the overall Top 3.


Regional ad-driven profit is heavily concentrated. The Central Region alone has generated approximately 23K MXN in cumulative ad-attributed profit — a clear signal of where the next campaign dollar earns the most.



Cumulative ad-driven profit by region — Central Region leads.

title: Numismática México — Year-End Holiday Sales Strategy
text:
Strategic Recommendations
Three campaigns, sequenced by risk and confidence:



The three-campaign playbook: star product, Top 5 replication, regional focus.



Prioritize the star product in high-revenue states. Highest-confidence move; the product has demonstrated price-insensitivity and a strong ad lift on a customer base that already recognizes the brand.
Replicate the January star-product playbook across the broader catalog. The proven-creative approach from the early-2022 campaign is the lowest-cost way to lift Top 5 products without rebuilding strategy from scratch.
Run a Top‑5 product campaign in high-revenue states. Combines (1) and

— uses the star product as the anchor and the Top 5 for cross-sell, targeting only the regions where the existing customer base already lifts ROAS.





Why each recommendation works — the underlying business levers.
